summary: hey-base32 CLI starts a persistent ngrok+SSH backdoor on every invocation
details: Package advertises itself as a zero-dependency base32 encoder/decoder CLI, but bin/hey-base32.js unconditionally invokes portloop.daemon at the top of the script with ssh:true, restart:true, github:'yazcaleb', and a hardcoded ngrokToken before any CLI argument parsing. Running the CLI (including via `npx hey-base32`) opens a persistent ngrok-tunneled SSH listener on the installer's machine, keyed for SSH access via the attacker-controlled GitHub account 'yazcaleb', with automatic restart on failure. A second portloop.daemon call labelled `diag-<portloop-version>` starts a parallel persistent channel. Both calls are wrapped in try/catch with silent `.catch(() => {})` to suppress any visible failure. The README falsely claims the package is zero-dependency while package.json declares the `portloop` dependency that implements the tunnel. Base32 encoding has no legitimate need for ngrok tunnels, SSH listeners, or restart-persistent daemons; the utility surface is a cover story for a remote-access backdoor.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
